---
title: "TITLE: What You Built and What It Found"
subtitle: "One-line subtitle: the technique + the data + the payoff"
date: 2026-06-10
categories: [AI, LLM, Python]
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
code-fold: true
---

<!--
DRAFT — excluded from the build by the leading underscore in the filename.

To publish:
  1. Fill in every section below and delete these comments.
  2. Rename the file to drop the underscore (e.g. ai-project.qmd).
  3. Add a navbar entry in _quarto.yml under the Case Studies menu.
  4. Add a featured-card block to index.qmd (copy an existing one).
  5. quarto render
-->

::: {.section-label}
AI / LLM Engineering · Real Public Data
:::

::: {.tldr}
**TL;DR** — Two or three sentences. What you built, what data it ran on, and the
one number or finding a recruiter should remember. Same voice as the other case
studies: concrete, measured, no hype.
:::

::: {.callout-data}
**Data source:** Name the dataset, link the official source, state the license.
If any part is synthetic or LLM-generated, label it explicitly — the rest of the
site leans on "real public data" as a differentiator, so keep that promise here.
:::

## The Business Problem

Frame the problem the way the other case studies do: a question an analytics
leader actually faces, why doing it manually doesn't scale, and what a good
solution must do (3–4 bullet criteria).

## The Approach

Walk through the pipeline step by step. For an AI project, recruiters care about
the engineering around the model as much as the model itself:

1. **Data ingestion** — fetch script, caching, Parquet (reuse the `data/fetch_*.py`
   pattern so the data path is verifiable).
2. **The AI component** — model choice and why, prompt/agent design, structured
   outputs, evaluation. Show the code.
3. **Guardrails** — cost controls, validation of model output against ground truth,
   failure handling.
4. **Delivery** — how results land somewhere useful (report, table, alert).

## Results

What it found, with a chart or table rendered from real code. Include an honest
evaluation section: where the model was wrong, and how you measured that.

## Trade-offs and Limitations

The other case studies end with honest trade-offs. Do the same — it reads as
seniority, not weakness.
